blocks.text: O come, let us sing to the Lord; let us make a joyful noise to the rock of our salvation!

Ps 95: Worship and obedience to the glorious Lord. A hymn celebrating the enthroned as supreme (see Ps 93n.). It invites Israel to sing (vv. 1–5), bow down before the (vv. 6–7c–), and become an obedient people (vv. 7d–11–). Themes and phrases of Pss 95–99 are repeated with variations.
1 : , a common metaphor for God—a massive cliff or mesa that serves as a refuge ( ).
Let us come into his presence with thanksgiving; let us make a joyful noise to him with songs of praise!
For the Lord is a great God, and a great King above all gods.
3 : , a title of suzerains in Mesopotamia, Egypt, and the Hittite kingdom.
In his hand are the depths of the earth; the heights of the mountains are his also.
The sea is his, for he made it, and the dry land, which his hands have formed.
O come, let us worship and bow down, let us kneel before the Lord, our Maker!
For he is our God, and we are the people of his pasture, and the sheep of his hand. O that today you would listen to his voice!
7 : connotes special love in that God does not delegate the care of Israel to others.
Do not harden your hearts, as at Meribah, as on the day at Massah in the wilderness,
8 : , a place in the wilderness, related by popular etymology to “place of strife”; , another place in the wilderness, in popular etymology, “place of testing”; for both see Ex 17.1–7; Num 21.1–9.
when your ancestors tested me, and put me to the proof, though they had seen my work.
For forty years I loathed that generation and said, “They are a people whose hearts go astray, and they do not regard my ways.”
Therefore in my anger I swore, “They shall not enter my rest.”
7d–11 : The tone and speaker change, and the community is told that as the ’s own flock, they are held to a high standard (cf. Pss 50; 81.8–9).
10–11 : God’s refusal to allow the first generation to enter the land ( ) is told in Num 14 and Deut 1.34–2:15; here it serves as a reminder for the community to obey God.
